trigger: Automated alert: error rate exceeded 20% and p99 latency exceeded 4s
description: Deliberately ambiguous incident: logs are dominated by memory/GC/OOM noise (a red herring), while the metrics show the true root cause. A single agent tends to anchor on the dramatic memory log lines and misdiagnose a memory leak; specialized agents that read metrics independently see that memory is bounded and stable, cache hit rate collapsed FIRST, and DB latency rose only afterward.
logs:
2024-03-02T03:09:40Z [INFO] cache-client: connection to cache-node-2 lost, entering fallback mode
2024-03-02T03:10:05Z [WARN] service-api: heap usage climbing: 2.6GB / 4GB
2024-03-02T03:10:20Z [WARN] service-api: GC pause 480ms (young gen), allocation rate elevated
2024-03-02T03:10:35Z [WARN] service-api: high memory pressure detected, request buffers accumulating
2024-03-02T03:10:50Z [ERROR] service-api: OOM candidate flagged by memory watchdog (heap 2.8GB)
2024-03-02T03:11:00Z [WARN] service-api: GC pause 610ms (full GC), throughput degraded
2024-03-02T03:11:15Z [ERROR] service-db: slow query: SELECT * FROM products WHERE category=? (execution_time: 2.9s)
2024-03-02T03:11:30Z [WARN] service-api: heap usage 2.8GB / 4GB, GC frequency increasing
2024-03-02T03:11:45Z [ERROR] service-api: request queue depth 640 (threshold 100), workers saturated
2024-03-02T03:12:00Z [ERROR] service-db: connection wait time 1.8s, active connections 250/300
2024-03-02T03:12:20Z [WARN] service-api: memory watchdog: sustained pressure, consider heap tuning
2024-03-02T03:12:40Z [ERROR] service-api: 503 errors climbing: 24% of requests failing
notes: Diagnostic tells: (1) memory rises modestly to 2.8GB and PLATEAUS well under the 4GB limit — not an unbounded leak; (2) db_connection_pool peaks at 251/300 — not exhausted; (3) cache_hit_rate collapses at 03:10 BEFORE db latency and error rate rise — cache failure is the leading indicator, DB overload and memory pressure are downstream symptoms.

ground_truth.root_cause: Cache node failure causing a cache-miss storm that overwhelmed the database
